data.title: Nutanix falls as ARR slightly misses expectations; fellow enterprise IT stocks mostly up
data.description:
Nutanix (NASDAQ:NTNX) shares plunged 8% by noon trading on Thursday, following fourth quarter fiscal 2025 results that beat estimates, but its guidance and annual recurring revenue came in just below market expectations.

"Early (DELL) PowerFlex wins a positive, especially with customers in the higher end of the pyramid," said Barclays analysts, led by Tim Long, in a Thursday investor note. "We expect NTNX to continue to see wins moving through FY26 and for the Pure Storage (PSTG) partnership to be additive. Management still views VMW share gain opportunity as in early innings, and a multi-year opp. Gross margin of 88.3% remains best in class."

However, Barclays also pointed out disappointing fiscal year 2026 guidance and concerning ARR commentary. The investment firm reiterated its Neutral rating and lowered its price target to $82 from $94.

Meanwhile, RBC Capital Markets maintained its Outperform rating and $95 price target.

"The disconnect once again was ARR missing expectations at $2,223M, +17% ($80M NNARR vs. $83M q/q and $88M y/y) vs. consensus of $2,241M as management doesn't guide to ARR but creates noise," said RBC analysts, led by Matthew Hedberg, in a note. "Broadly, demand trends are encouraging though Fed remains pressured resulting in longer deal cycles as this has been factored into guidance."

Needham retained its Buy rating but lowered its price target to $80 from $92 due to ARR headwinds. However, it was encouraged that Nutanix should start to take market share from Broadcom's (AVGO) VMware in the quarters ahead.

KeyBanc reiterated its Overweight rating and $95 price target.

"We think results highlight growing evidence of NTNX's importance to the ecosystem, continued opportunity with VMware displacements, improved go-to-market initiatives with CSPs and Cisco (CSCO), and integration opportunities with OEM, best shown in accelerating customer net additions," said KeyBanc analysts Brandon Nispel and Matt Sbriglio in a note.

Shares of other enterprise IT stocks provided mixed reactions by noon trading, but most were higher. NetApp (NTAP) had increased 2.8%, IBM (IBM) inched up 0.1%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) climbed 2.3%, Microsoft (MSFT) edged up 0.4% and Broadcom added 2.5%.

data.title: Mortgage rates hit ten-month low, purchase demand up
data.description:
Mortgage rates reached a ten-month low, leading to a rise in purchase demand, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.56% as of August 28, down from 6.58% last week and higher than 6.35% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.69%, unchanged from a week ago and up from 5.51% a year ago.

"Purchase demand continues to rise on the back of lower rates and solid economic growth," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac’s chief economist.

"Though many potential homebuyers still face affordability challenges, consistently lower rates may provide them with the impetus to enter the market," added Khater.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the last ten months:

data.title: Niles Management’s Dan Niles: ‘This market is addicted to easy money’
data.description:
Despite Nvidia (NVDA) reporting earnings that beat top and bottom line estimates, its shares fell slightly as data center revenues missed expectations, intensifying fears about Chinese competition. Dan Niles, founder and portfolio manager at Niles Investment Management, expressed concerns during a CNBC interview about the sustainability of current market valuations, particularly in the AI sector (NYSEARCA:AIEQ), (ARTY).

“If you are honest with yourself, you look at this and say, ‘they actually missed data center revenues for the first time since any of us heard about ChatGPT,’” Niles stated, contradicting his earlier bullish stance on the company.

He pointed to several concerning signals in the AI space, including Sam Altman’s comments about an AI bubble and ChatGPT-5’s disappointing performance.

Niles emphasized that the market’s positive reaction to otherwise concerning news stems primarily from anticipated Federal Reserve rate cuts. “This market is addicted to easy money. And in that environment, you’re paid to just take risks because you’re not really going to get punished,” he said, suggesting that monetary policy is currently overshadowing fundamental business metrics.

He highlighted troubling data about AI investment returns, noting that “95% of corporations investing in AI are seeing 0% returns” according to an MIT study. He also pointed to Meta’s (META) hiring freeze following an earlier hiring surge as further evidence of cooling enthusiasm for AI investments among major tech companies (MAGS).

Drawing parallels to the dot-com era, Niles compared the current market environment to 1999 when the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) surged before its eventual collapse. “The four biggest hyperscalers – (MSFT), (AMZN), (GOOGL), (META) – have had their capex double to $600B over the last two years,” he said, warning that while the market currently ignores valuation concerns, investors will eventually need to differentiate between AI’s winners and losers.

The veteran semiconductor analyst concluded that while current monetary conditions support continued market optimism, more pragmatic considerations about returns on AI investments will eventually matter.

“Right now, the market just doesn’t care,” he said, suggesting that this indifference to fundamentals won’t last indefinitely.

data.title: Phillips 66 to start winding down Los Angeles refinery next week - Reuters
data.description:
Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) will begin winding down operations at its 139K bbl/day Los Angeles-area refinery for permanent closure as early as next week, Reuters reported Thursday.

"The refinery units will begin idling by Q4 2025 as planned... our timeline remains unchanged," a company spokesperson told Reuters, without providing a more detailed timeline but noting the process of idling the facilities requires a complex, multi-phased approach.

The refinery employs ~600 workers and 300 contractors, with most of them expected to be laid off by December and a few retained workers to be transferred to the company's Los Angeles marine oil terminal.

data.title: Movado outlines $10M in annualized savings while navigating new 39% Swiss import tariffs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Movado Group, Inc. (MOV) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Efraim Grinberg, Chairman & CEO, stated that "we are pleased with our overall results this quarter as we return to growth in both sales and profitability. Sales grew by 3% to $161.8 million and adjusted operating profit more than doubled to $7 million from $2.6 million last year despite a $2.2 million impact from unmitigated U.S. tariff expenses." Grinberg highlighted the implementation of a new 39% U.S. tariff on Swiss imports and reported that the company has built a strong inventory position in Swiss-made watches to cover a substantial portion of the year's needs. He addressed strategic pricing actions to offset tariffs, most of which became effective July 1. Grinberg also noted "approximately $10 million of annualized savings spread evenly throughout this year as a result of the actions we took late last year to reduce operating expenses."
  • Grinberg explained that licensed brands grew by 6.5% on a constant currency basis and 9.5% on a reported basis, while the Movado brand experienced a 5.6% sales decline. He pointed to strong international business performance, especially in Europe, Latin America, and India, with Europe seeing particularly strong trends. The U.S. business declined by 1.6%, although domestic department store and e-commerce channels improved.
  • On the product front, Grinberg shared that "Movado has seen increased penetration and success in women's watches, including our new iconic bangle watches and our new Mini Quest in BOLD, which along with our BOLD tank watch, is the best seller. On the men's side, we're seeing strong performance in the Movado BOLD collection, including Verso Automatic and Quest Automatic."
  • Sallie A. DeMarsilis, Executive VP & CFO, stated: "Sales were $161.8 million as compared to $157 million last year, an increase of 3.1%. In constant dollars, the increase in net sales was 1.4%. Net sales increased across licensed brands and company stores partially offset by a decrease in net sales in owned brands."

Outlook

  • Grinberg remarked that "we would expect to see approximately $10 million of annualized savings spread evenly throughout this year as a result of the actions we took late last year to reduce operating expenses." He acknowledged ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and the broader retail environment but expressed optimism about new products and the resurgence in the fashion watch market.
  • Management reiterated that due to macroeconomic and tariff-related uncertainty, "the company is not providing fiscal 2026 outlook."

Financial Results

  • DeMarsilis reported that "operating expenses were $80.6 million as compared to $82.6 million for the second quarter of last year. The $2 million decrease was driven by a strategic reduction in marketing expenses, partially offset by an increase in performance-based compensation."
  • Gross profit as a percent of sales was 54.1% compared to 54.3% in the second quarter of last year. DeMarsilis explained, "the decrease in gross margin rate as compared to the same period of last year was primarily driven by increased tariffs and unfavorable foreign exchange, partially offset by favorable channel and product mix."
  • Net income in the second quarter was $5.3 million or $0.23 per diluted share as compared to $3.5 million or $0.15 per diluted share in the year ago period.
  • Cash at the end of the second quarter was $180.5 million. Inventory was up $28.3 million or 15.5% above the same period of last year, with $4.6 million of reciprocal tariffs included in inventory on hand.

Q&A

  • Hamed Khorsand, BWS Financial: Asked about the "mini" watch trend and its impact on consumer habits. Grinberg responded that the trend toward smaller watches "has actually brought young women back into the category, and there's a lot of social media around that and layering of women's watches with jewelry."
  • Khorsand questioned participation in Prime Day and performance. Grinberg indicated that "we've seen our overall digital business with those retailers that are completely focused on digital environment, whether it be Zalandos or the Amazons of the world, really doing very well on a global basis."
  • Khorsand inquired about inventory build and digestion by year-end. Grinberg said inventories were rebuilt after being low at year-end and should be in line by year-end, aiding tariff mitigation. DeMarsilis added that "about $28 million of additional inventory at this time. We do expect to work it down by the end of the year to something more reasonable. But of that $18 million, about $16 million of it is in the U.S."
  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen: Asked about the timeline for restructuring charges. Grinberg responded, "I would think on the restructuring side, they're predominantly done and there could be some laggard still expenses on the other charges. But I would expect overall that they will be reduced significantly."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on inventory, restructuring, and consumer trends, with a generally neutral tone focused on clarity and risk management.
  • Management maintained a slightly positive and confident tone in prepared remarks, shifting to detailed and explanatory responses in Q&A. Grinberg expressed confidence in mitigation strategies and product trends.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained neutral and focused on operational clarity, while management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to increased confidence supported by tangible results and mitigation actions. Grinberg stated, "we're confident that we're taking the right actions for the long term and positioning Movado Group for sustainable success."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw sales growth and improved profitability versus the previous quarter, where sales had declined. Operating profit rebounded from $0.9 million to $7 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • Management's commentary moved from cautious optimism and focus on resilience and flexibility in Q1 to greater confidence about strategic execution and savings in Q2.
  • Both quarters refrained from providing fiscal outlook due to similar macroeconomic and tariff uncertainties, but Q2 emphasized the success of mitigation strategies and operational improvements.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from seeking explanations for sales momentum and cash flow alignment in Q1 to operational specifics on inventory and restructuring in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing risks from U.S. tariffs on Swiss-made watches, with the recent implementation of a 39% tariff rate.
  • Strategies to mitigate tariff impact include inventory build-up, price actions, and supplier negotiations.
  • Macroeconomic conditions and foreign exchange volatility remain concerns affecting gross margin and inventory levels.
  • Analysts raised questions about inventory management, the impact of tariffs, and the completion of restructuring-related charges.

Final Takeaway

Movado Group returned to top- and bottom-line growth in the second quarter, supported by strategic savings initiatives and strong results in licensed brands and international markets, even as new U.S. tariffs on Swiss imports present ongoing challenges. Management expressed confidence in mitigation strategies and product innovation, maintaining a strong balance sheet while continuing to navigate an uncertain macroeconomic and tariff environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Victoria's Secret raises full-year net sales guidance to $6.41B amid strong Q2 and strategic brand momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Hillary Super opened by highlighting continued momentum in the second quarter, "once again delivering results that beat our top and bottom line guidance." She emphasized sequential improvements, "We grew net sales 3% despite the digital outage in May and grew comp sales 4%, both sequential improvements over the prior quarter and above our expectations."
  • Super detailed progress on the Path to Potential strategy, noting, "Our refreshed leadership team is in place, including a new Chief Marketing and Customer Officer and a new Brand President model." She stressed increased innovation and faster production cycles, citing the successful Body by Victoria FlexFactor bra launch, which "saw double-digit new customer growth at Victoria's Secret in the last week of the month when the campaign began."
  • Super described international growth as a standout, with "Q2 net sales grew 22% year-over-year while retail comps were up high single digits." She highlighted the impact of collaborations, stating the PINK x LoveShackFancy collection was "a record-breaking collaboration for both PINK and LoveShackFancy" and noted, "Strong traffic and exceptionally large basket sizes drove outsized results, all achieved with 0 discounting."
  • CFO Scott Sekella stated, "Our second quarter results exceeded expectations, building on our strong first quarter beat." He added, "Net sales were up $42 million or 3% year-over-year while comps grew 4%." Sekella also noted, "Adjusted operating income of $55 million exceeded the high end of our guidance by $20 million."

Outlook

  • The company raised its full year outlook for net sales, now forecasting net sales in the range of $6.33 billion to $6.41 billion, up from previous guidance of $6.2 billion to $6.3 billion.
  • Sekella stated, "Given the outperformance during the second quarter was offset by incremental tariff exposure, we are maintaining our adjusted operating income range for fiscal year 2025 of $270 million to $320 million."
  • Guidance for fiscal year 2025 adjusted net income per diluted share remains at $1.80 to $2.20.
  • Third quarter net sales are forecast in the range of $1.39 billion to $1.42 billion.
  • Third quarter adjusted net loss per share is expected to be in the range of (-$0.55) to (-$0.75).

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q2 were $1.459 billion, up 3% versus last year.
  • Adjusted gross margin rate was 35.6%, 20 basis points above last year and 60 basis points above guidance.
  • Adjusted operating income reached $55 million.
  • Adjusted net income per diluted share came in at $0.33.
  • International reported sales grew 22% to $228 million in the quarter.
  • Cash balance at quarter-end was $188 million, with an outstanding balance under the $750 million ABL credit facility at $75 million.
  • Total inventories ended the quarter up 4% compared to last year, in line with guidance.

Q&A

  • Lauren B. Levine, Morgan Stanley, asked about the end of the de minimis exemption. CFO Sekella responded, "The de minimis exemption isn't a big part of how we go to market."
  • Marni Shapiro, The Retail Tracker, inquired about innovation. Super replied, "We have a very deep pipeline of innovation, primarily focused on bras but also in sport... you'll see a more constant drumbeat from us in both innovation and in fashion as we move through the back half of this year and into next year."
  • Dana Lauren Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group, questioned gross margin strategy and Store of the Future performance. Sekella explained, "We've really made a conscious effort to pull back on promos and the traditional discounts. We expect that to continue into the back half."
  • Michael Vu, Barclays, asked about international category performance. Super said, "Intimates business is very strong there... it's really the 2 core categories of Beauty as well as intimates both doing very well globally."
  • Mauricio Serna Vega, UBS, requested details on tariffs. Sekella clarified, "Our net tariff impact in fiscal '25 is $100 million total. There is $70 million of mitigation in that... mitigation will definitely increase in 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were largely positive, focusing on innovation, international growth, and gross margin management, with recurring praise for new product launches and leadership changes.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and optimistic, repeatedly using language such as "momentum," "excited," and "progress," and emphasizing strategic discipline and forward momentum.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management demonstrated increased optimism, as the company reported sequential improvements and raised guidance.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was upbeat, and remained positive and engaged during Q&A, with direct answers and forward-looking enthusiasm.
  • Analysts showed more enthusiasm this quarter, with a shift from caution in Q1 to more congratulatory and opportunity-focused dialogue in Q2.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw raised net sales guidance and sequential improvement in both net sales and comp sales, as opposed to the flat net sales and down 1% comps reported in Q1.
  • International growth accelerated from high single-digit growth in Q1 to 22% reported sales growth in Q2.
  • Management's language shifted from cautious optimism to confident execution, with greater emphasis on innovation cadence and brand momentum.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from team structure and tariff mitigation in Q1 to innovation, international strategy, and gross margin leverage in Q2.
  • The previous quarter highlighted new leadership appointments, while the current quarter focused on the impact of those leaders and their strategies in action.
  • Tariff headwinds increased in Q2 compared to Q1, with incremental exposure offset by mitigation efforts and a maintained operating income range.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and increased tariff pressures, with guidance now reflecting a 30% tariff for China and 20% for non-China imports.
  • The company addressed mitigation efforts, including "optimizing costs with vendors, further diversifying our sourcing, ensuring we have the most efficient air-versus-ocean freight mix possible and implementing a combination of select pricing adjustments."
  • The digital security incident in May was noted as having a negative impact on net sales of approximately $20 million and an operating income impact of approximately $14 million in the quarter.

Final Takeaway

Victoria's Secret & Co. reported a strong second quarter with sequential improvements and raised its full-year net sales outlook, highlighting accelerating international growth, robust innovation in core categories, and continued progress on its Path to Potential strategy. Gross margin expansion, disciplined cost management, and effective tariff mitigation supported results, while new leadership and strategic collaborations drove higher engagement and new customer acquisition. The company remains focused on executing key brand moments and product launches, maintaining confidence in its ability to deliver value for shareholders amid a dynamic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IQVIA partners with Flagship Pioneering to boost biotech development
data.description:
• IQVIA (NYSE:IQV) on Thursday said in a statement that it would collaborate with Flagship Pioneering to accelerate the development of breakthrough life sciences companies.

  • The deal would give Flagship’s ecosystem access to IQVIA’s AI-driven analytics, clinical development capabilities, and real-world data infrastructure across the entire drug lifecycle.

  • The companies would jointly focus on drug development strategy and analytics, clinical development, and asset valuation and due diligence.
  • IQV is -1.29% to $186.35
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cool Company signals market balance by 2027 with 50% charter coverage amid vessel upgrades
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cool Company Ltd. (CLCO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Patrick St Lawrence Tyrrell reported that average TCE was slightly down at $69,900 per day, while total operating revenue remained steady at $85.5 million and adjusted EBITDA rose to $56.5 million, supported by the delivery of Kool Tiger and GAIL Sagar. Tyrrell noted, "We had to work hard to fix vessels that have rolled off charter, often fixing them multiple times in the spot market at unsatisfactory rates that reflect competition from the glut of ships in the market."
  • Tyrrell emphasized the company's charter backlog as a stabilizing force, stating, "Our results are very much underpinned by our backlog in this type of environment."
  • The CEO highlighted positive LNG supply developments and projected, "We see a 23% and 39% increase in LNG supply compared to 2024 volumes by the end of 2026 and the end of 2028, respectively."
  • Tyrrell addressed the market cycle, saying, "What's remarkable about this year so far is how orders have fallen behind the supply curve. This was inevitable given the market and at the newbuilding prices that remain suddenly high, and it's a signal of a natural balancing that is positive for CoolCo's fleet longer term."
  • Tyrrell provided guidance on vessels rolling off long-term charters: "We're likely to be down well over $100,000 per day across these 2 vessels come Q4, depending on how spot rates develop."
  • CFO Johannes P. Boots stated, "We reported total operating revenues of $85.5 million, in line with the prior quarter and above the guidance provided during our last call."
  • Boots added, "Net income for Q2 of $11.9 million is an increase of $2.8 million versus Q1, which was also impacted by less unrealized interest rate losses on the swaps."

Outlook

  • Management anticipates, "For Q3, we anticipate total operating revenues to be at a similar level as Q2."
  • Tyrrell explained that upcoming vessel redeliveries will impact average TCE rates, noting, "Towards the end of the quarter, 2 vessels will be redelivered from their existing contracts, but each with a first spot voyage already secured. This will obviously impact our average TCE rate going forward as well."
  • Tyrrell stated, "50% of our days are covered until 2027, by which time we anticipate the return of a much more balanced market, of course, with sentiment returning in advance."

Financial Results

  • Boots reported, "Fleet-wide time and voyage charter revenues translated to an average TCE of $69,900 per day in Q2 versus $70,600 in Q1."
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $56.5 million compared to $53.4 million in Q1, with Boots noting the increase was "largely reflecting the absence of the voyage-related expenses tied to the newbuilds in January this year."
  • Boots highlighted improved vessel operating expenses: "In the current quarter, average vessel operating expenses were $15,900 per day across the fleet of 13 vessels, a decrease from both Q1 and also from the average 2004 run rate, which was approximately $17,300 per day."
  • The company ended the quarter with $109 million in cash and $226 million in total available liquidity, and repurchased 859,000 shares at an average price of $5.77 per share, reducing the share count by 1.6%.
  • Boots also shared, "Approximately 75% of our total notional debt is hedged or fixed. But if you adjust for net debt rather than gross debt, that coverage ratio increases further to 82%."

Q&A

  • Alexander Bidwell, Webber Research Advisory: Asked about the impact of recent liquefaction activity on charter market sentiment. Tyrrell responded, "It's early days and the people who are sort of coming into the market now, I think they're bottom fishing a little bit. But certainly, this positive news flow is starting to get people focused on their long-term shipping needs."
  • Bidwell inquired about potential asset acquisitions. Boots replied, "No, nothing in particular at this point. We're always looking at acquisitions, whether it's companies and vessels. So it's more a continued effort to look at these opportunities, but nothing concrete."
  • Liam Dalton Burke, B. Riley: Queried about returns on vessel upgrades. Tyrrell said, "That's been a pretty good return. Investment was around $10 million. So we're going to get the -- well, at the moment, at least, we're getting the $5,000 per day. I think we could potentially get a little bit more than that."
  • Burke asked about drydocking scheduling. Tyrrell stated, "We always drydocked in the shelter seasons where possible. And that's no different from what it would be otherwise."
  • Frode Morkedal, Clarksons Securities: Asked about LNG-E upgrades. Tyrrell explained, "4 out of 5 are completed. So there's 1 more which will take place in the fourth quarter. They did cost about... $10 million each for the sub, call it."
  • Petter Haugen, ABG: Inquired about 3-year variable charter metrics and upgrade premiums. Tyrrell confirmed, "It is tied to an index... the floor excluding the upside from the sub-cooler and the other on grades is 20 and then the ceiling is 100 plus or minus." On the upgrade premium, "We're getting $5,000 a day, the actual upside is $10,000 a day."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to cautiously optimistic tone, focusing on asset returns, upgrade premiums, and long-term market balance. Their questions probed for clarity on near-term challenges and upside potential.
  • Management's tone was measured and pragmatic, acknowledging market headwinds but repeatedly emphasizing backlog protection and strategic flexibility. Tyrrell conveyed confidence by stating, "Our backlog provides a cushion," while Boots highlighted, "Our financial position remains solid, giving us both stability and the flexibility to pursue growth when opportunities arise."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management remains steady, with continued focus on market recovery timing and capital efficiency. The tone in Q2 was slightly more confident regarding financial stability and cost management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained steady revenue and improved adjusted EBITDA quarter-over-quarter, with net income increasing from $9.1 million in Q1 to $11.9 million in Q2.
  • Average TCE declined from $70,600 in Q1 to $69,900 in Q2, reflecting ongoing spot market pressures.
  • Vessel operating expenses improved, dropping to $15,900 per day from $16,300 in the prior quarter.
  • Management reiterated the importance of backlog and market balance, now covering 50% of days through 2027 versus higher coverage levels in Q1.
  • Analyst focus shifted from macro LNG market supply growth and vessel ordering in Q1 to operational execution, vessel upgrades, and charter strategy in Q2.
  • Management's confidence in backlog and liquidity remains, with new emphasis on capital structure optimization and share repurchases.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tyrrell cited ongoing spot market weakness and vessel oversupply as near-term challenges, particularly for vessels rolling off charters and entering the spot market at lower rates.
  • The CEO underscored the risk of additional vessel idling, stating, "The immediate market backdrop obviously isn't as good as the macro picture."
  • Boots signaled prudent management of interest rate risk and a cautious approach to further asset acquisitions, emphasizing "continued effort to look at these opportunities, but nothing concrete."
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sustainability of upgrade premiums and the timing of improved charter rates.

Final Takeaway

Cool Company Ltd. emphasized that its charter backlog and disciplined capital management are providing resilience in a challenging LNG shipping market. With 50% of vessel days covered through 2027 and continued investment in vessel upgrades, management anticipates a more balanced market as new LNG supply comes online. The company highlighted operational improvements, strong liquidity, and a prudent approach to asset growth, while remaining focused on navigating current spot market pressures and securing long-term value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cooper Companies outlines $2B free cash flow target over next 3 years while advancing MyDAY and MiSight launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cooper Companies (COO) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Albert G. White reported, "Q3 consolidated revenues were up 5.7% year-over-year or up 2% organically to $1.06 billion. Margins improved and non-GAAP earnings grew double digits to $1.10, up 15% year-over-year. Free cash flow was strong at $165 million, and we repurchased $52 million of our stock during the quarter."
  • White noted that while revenues were lower than expected, "we delivered strong margins, double-digit earnings growth and robust free cash flow, reflecting the operational excellence that remains central to our growth strategy."
  • For CooperVision, White stated, "we reported revenues of $718 million for the quarter, reflecting 6.3% reported growth and 2.4% organic growth." White attributed revenue softness to "clariti declined globally, led by a noticeable drop in Asia Pac and a slowdown in the Americas and EMEA" and "greater-than-expected weakness within the pure-play e-commerce segment in Asia Pac, excluding Japan."
  • White highlighted that MyDAY grew double digits with "multifocal grew 20% as this fantastic lens continues to perform extremely well" and added, "MyDAY Energys...we expect to launch in Europe in early fiscal 2026; MyDAY multifocal, which we expect to launch in several major APAC markets soon...and our MyDAY toric parameter expansion, which is actively being rolled out in multiple markets now."
  • The CEO explained that MiSight "grew 23%" and "we just received final regulatory approval for MiSight to launch in Japan, and commercialization is planned for early 2026."
  • For CooperSurgical, quarterly revenues were $342 million, up 4.5% or up 2% organically. Fertility revenues totaled $137 million, growing 6% or up 3% organically. White mentioned, "we're still seeing signs of pressure on the market with clinics continuing to manage cash conservatively by delaying capital purchases and installations along with ongoing softness in cycles in Asia Pac."
  • White said, "we remain confident in our ability to deliver sustainable revenue growth and gain market share" looking ahead to fiscal 2026 and expects "operating margin expansion as we lever prior investment activity and a more efficient organization."
  • CFO Brian G. Andrews stated, "Gross margin improved by 70 basis points to 67.3%, driven by continued efficiency gains, mix and positive foreign exchange. Operating expenses grew in line with sales, reflecting disciplined cost management."
  • Andrews also reported, "Non-GAAP EPS was $1.10, up 15% based on approximately 200 million average shares outstanding. Free cash flow was $165 million with CapEx of $97 million. Net debt declined to $2.35 billion and our bank defined leverage ratio improved to 1.77x."
  • Andrews outlined, "we expect to generate approximately $2 billion in free cash flow over the next 3 fiscal years."

Outlook

  • Andrews provided Q4 consolidated revenue guidance of $1.049 billion to $1.069 billion, representing 2% to 4% organic growth. CooperVision's revenue is expected to be in the range of $700 million to $713 million, up 2% to 4% organically, and CooperSurgical revenue is expected to be $350 million to $356 million, up 2% to 4% organically.
  • For earnings, guidance is non-GAAP EPS of $1.10 to $1.14.
  • Management expects to generate roughly $100 million in Q4 free cash flow, bringing the full year to about $385 million.
  • For fiscal 2026, Andrews said, "we expect the impact [of tariffs] to be approximately $24 million, lower than previously anticipated," with plans to offset this through cost management and ongoing productivity initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Reported Q3 consolidated revenues were $1.06 billion, gross margin was 67.3%, operating margin expanded to 26.1%, and free cash flow was $165 million.
  • CapEx was $97 million, net debt declined to $2.35 billion, and the bank defined leverage ratio improved to 1.77x.
  • The company repurchased 724,000 shares for $52.1 million, and $164 million remains available under the repurchase plan.

Q&A

  • Jonathan David Block, Stifel: Asked about CVI's growth relative to the market and the impact of MiSight. CEO White responded, "our portfolio has been lagging the market...because we didn't have full availability of MyDAY...that's changed." Block followed up on clariti and MyDAY margin dynamics. White explained, "in some geographies...clariti [and] MyDAY...were almost viewed as somewhat similar type of products...a number of optometrists...saying, 'Hey, I'm going to hold off for a little while on clariti...try this MyDAY.'" Andrews confirmed, "clariti's margins are a little bit better than MyDAY's."
  • Lawrence H. Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Inquired about slowing market growth. White replied, "the market grew 7% last year. It's grown 4% so far this year...you're seeing some of the pricing come off the market a little bit."
  • Jeffrey D. Johnson, Baird: Asked about the timing of MyDAY replacing clariti and e-commerce pricing pressures. White said, "the guidance that we gave assumes a very similar quarter, if you will, Q3 to Q4...we wanted to ensure that we gave guidance that was certainly reasonable..." and regarding e-commerce, "we've seen some more aggressive pricing...outside of the U.S. and certainly in the Asia Pac region."
  • Issie Kirby, Redburn: Asked about restructuring. White replied, "we're now taking a fresh look...to make it as efficient as possible moving forward...we've invested very considerably over the last several years to put us in a position where we can complete that analysis and take some appropriate action."
  • Joanne Karen Wuensch, Citigroup: Sought clarity on how CVI reaccelerates. White said, "it's almost all tied to MyDAY...we have over 30 brand-new MyDAY private label contracts and launches going on right now."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed slightly negative to neutral sentiment, focusing on clariti softness, market share dynamics, margin questions, and uncertainty around the MyDAY ramp. Repeated inquiries probed the timing and magnitude of product transitions and market growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, but responses to analyst questions were more measured and occasionally defensive, using phrases like "we cannot get in front of ourselves" and "that's a question mark, right?" The positive sentiment from earlier prepared remarks was tempered in the Q&A by candid discussions of risks and delays.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more defensive and realistic stance, with both management and analysts more focused on near-term headwinds and the timing of improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q4 organic growth is more conservative (2% to 4%) versus prior expectations of stronger back-half growth. Previous quarter guidance reflected optimism for a revenue acceleration in Q4, while current guidance explicitly factors in risks from clariti and e-commerce channels.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to managing transitions between product lines (clariti to MyDAY) and regional market pressures, particularly in Asia Pac. The earlier bullish tone on MyDAY's capacity and product launches remains but is paired with caution about the pace of revenue conversion.
  • Analysts have shifted their focus from longer-term market share gains to near-term execution and the sustainability of product transitions. Management's confidence in operating leverage and free cash flow has increased, as evidenced by the new $2 billion free cash flow target over three years.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing headwinds from clariti softness, e-commerce channel pricing pressures in Asia Pac, and delayed capital spending in fertility clinics.
  • Guidance incorporates risk of continued clariti order lag and slow revenue conversion from MyDAY fitting activity.
  • Analysts consistently questioned the timing and scale of MyDAY's revenue impact, potential for further clariti declines, and the implications for market share.
  • Management is executing restructuring and productivity initiatives to mitigate gross margin and tariff pressures.

Final Takeaway

Cooper Companies delivered double-digit earnings growth, expanding margins and strong free cash flow, yet acknowledged softer-than-expected revenue due to clariti headwinds and Asia Pac e-commerce challenges. Management is guiding conservatively for Q4, prioritizing disciplined cost control, and expects to generate approximately $2 billion in free cash flow over the next three years. The company is confident about the upcoming MyDAY and MiSight launches, recent private label wins, and operating margin expansion, but recognizes the need for continued operational execution to navigate near-term pressures and realize the full revenue potential from current initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tariffs turn Rio Tinto into aluminum buyer in U.S. market - Bloomberg
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) is buying aluminum in the U.S. and reselling it to American customers rather than moving its own metal from Canada, as it is now cheaper due to President Trump's 50% tariff on aluminum imports, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The tariff, which took effect in June, has forced Rio (NYSE:RIO) to reduce shipments to the U.S. and instead buy supplies from rivals, as long as those supplies are already in the country, the report said.

The tariff has roiled North America's highly integrated metals market, while pushing aluminum prices (LMAHDS03:COM) well above other international markets since January due to the tariff threat, with the Midwest premium jumping 81% since early June.

Rio (RIO), which runs a refinery and five smelters in Quebec, reportedly has bought at least 50K tons of aluminum from the U.S. spot market since June, while it shipped 723K tons of aluminum to the U.S. in H1, most of it passing before the 50% levies took effect.

Rio (RIO) has said the tariffs on its Canadian-made aluminum resulted in gross costs of $321M in this year's H1; Alcoa (NYSE:AA), the largest U.S. producer, said in July that U.S. tariffs on Canadian shipments in H1 took a $135M bite out of the company's profits.

Canada is the biggest foreign supplier of aluminum to the U.S., accounting for 53% of imports of the metal in the past 12 months.

Other potentially relevant stocks include Century Aluminum (CENX) and Kaiser Aluminum (KALU).
